title: Decorator Factories with Arguments

overview.why: It enables configurable decorators. Instead of writing separate decorators for different behaviors, you write one configurable factory.

why_learning_this: Allows you to write dynamic and highly reusable decorators.
Required to understand how modern Python web frameworks handle routing and configuration.

common_mistakes.title: Missing a level of nesting
common_mistakes.explanation: Writing a factory with only two levels (factory and wrapper) instead of three. The factory must return a decorator, which in turn returns a wrapper.

common_mistakes.title: Applying the factory without calling it
common_mistakes.explanation: Writing `@retry` instead of `@retry()`. Even if the factory has default arguments, you must call it with parentheses so it executes and returns the actual decorator.

recall_questions.q: Why is a third level of nesting required when creating a decorator that takes arguments?
recall_questions.answer: Because the standard decorator syntax automatically passes the target function as the single argument. The outermost factory function is needed to capture the custom configuration arguments and return the actual decorator that will receive the target function.

recall_questions.q: What does the outermost function in a decorator factory return?
recall_questions.answer: It returns the middle function, which acts as the actual decorator.

understanding_checks.question: What happens when you run this code?
understanding_checks.answer: A TypeError is raised, or it behaves completely incorrectly (it actually returns the `decorator` function).
understanding_checks.why: The syntax `@prefix` means `say_hello = prefix(say_hello)`. `prefix` is a factory expecting the string `p`, but it receives the function `say_hello`. It returns `decorator`, meaning `say_hello` is now the `decorator` function itself. Decorator factories must be called: `@prefix('Hello')`.

understanding_checks.question: When defining a decorator factory, why do we need three levels of nested functions instead of two?
understanding_checks.answer: Because the factory needs to take the configuration arguments, return a decorator that takes the target function, which in turn returns the wrapper that takes the runtime arguments.
understanding_checks.why: Standard decorators take the function automatically. A factory function isn't a decorator itself; it's a function that returns a decorator. Thus, Level 1: take config arguments, Level 2: take target function, Level 3: take target function's arguments.

practice_tasks.name: Add Configuration to a Decorator
practice_tasks.description: We have a basic `@retry` decorator that retries a function 3 times. Modify it into a decorator factory `retry(max_retries)` so we can configure the number of retries per function.

aha_moment.prediction: What is the exact order of the printed lines when this script runs?
aha_moment.common_guess:
App started
Registering /home
Decorator attached
Handling request
aha_moment.why: The factory and decorator run at module load time to register the route before the app even starts. The wrapper only runs when a client actually makes a request, which doesn't happen here.

challenge.title: Rate Limiting Factory
challenge.prompt: Create a decorator factory `rate_limit` that accepts an integer `max_requests`. It should decorate a function representing an API endpoint. If the endpoint is called more than `max_requests` times, the wrapper should return the string `'Rate limit exceeded'`. Otherwise, it should increment a counter and return the function's result. You can use an attribute on the wrapper itself to store the call count.
